title: Today's AI Brief: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab
summary: Exploring AI prototypes from Google's Futures Lab and an AI startup cleaning homes for free
imageAlt: Image from source article for Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.
imageCaption: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.
imageSourceTitle: Check out real-life AI prototypes from the Futures Lab.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and I'm excited to share some fresh AI developments with you. First up, Google's Futures Lab has been working on some fascinating AI prototypes. I am looking at stories coming out of the University of Waterloo, where students have built custom AI-generated stories to help learn languages like Japanese. It's not just about language learning; they're also experimenting with AI tutors for sign language, providing real-time feedback. Imagine being able to practice sign language with an AI tutor that corrects your handshapes and finger placement on the fly - it's a game-changer for accessibility.

Next, an AI startup called Shift is taking a unique approach to training future robots. They're offering free home cleaning services in exchange for data to train their robots. The idea is to use a 'magic hat' to record their cleaners at work inside your home. My read is that this is an interesting way to gather real-world data for AI training, but it does raise some eyebrows about data boundaries and safety. I mean, who wouldn't want a free cleaning, but do we want a 'magic hat' snooping around our living rooms?

Another aspect that's got me thinking is the potential for these robots to learn from real-world environments. Shift's approach could help robots adapt to different cleaning tasks, from dusting to vacuuming, and make them more efficient in their work. It's a clever strategy, but it's also a reminder that AI is becoming increasingly intertwined with our daily lives.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.
